scope_note: QA Orbit-Lattice Mod-3 Collapse Cert [515]. Primary sources: Hoffstein, Pipher, Silverman (1998) 'NTRU: A Ring-Based Public Key Cryptosystem' ANTS-III LNCS 1423 DOI 10.1007/BFb0054868 (NTRU construction and attack lattice); Lenstra, Lenstra, Lovasz (1982) 'Factoring polynomials with rational coefficients' Math. Annalen 261 DOI 10.1007/BF01457454 (LLL reduction); fpylll dev team (2024) v0.6.4 https://github.com/fplll/fpylll (reduction implementation used for the empirical record). CLAIM (narrow, falsifiable): deriving NTRU-style small-coefficient key polynomials from QA orbit sequences via coefficient=(b mod 3)-1 is cryptographically unsafe whenever the QA modulus m is divisible by 3, and is not measurably weaker than a random key when gcd(m,3)=1. ROOT CAUSE (proved): qa_step(b,e,m) reduces b+e-1 mod m; whenever 3|m, (x mod m) === x (mod 3), so the mod-3 residue of the orbit evolves exactly as the direct mod-3 recursion, which has intrinsic period at most 8 regardless of m's own period -- any polynomial of degree N>8 built this way is periodic with period<=8. Verified exactly (not approximately) for m in {9,15,21,30,300}. EMPIRICAL RECORD (2026-07-04, fpylll 0.6.4, N=83 q=256 real NTRU lattice, real LLL/BKZ, not narrated): random keys 0/10 broken by LLL (avg ratio ~594); QA keys m=9 (3|9) 6/10 broken (ratios as low as 0.01-0.24); QA keys m=80 (gcd(80,3)=1) 0-1/12 broken, statistically matching random; under BKZ block_size 10/20/30 both random and gcd(m,3)=1 QA keys break equally (8/8, avg ratio ~1.0) -- N=83 is simply too small for any NTRU instance to resist BKZ, confirming the safe construction fails the same way as random under a stronger attack too, not just LLL. This corrects a specific prior claim: informal chat-derived 'QARSDC'/'QAFST' cryptography material asserted QA 'harmonic class filtering' mitigates lattice attacks, but never specified an actual lattice basis (literally 'L=span{...}', unfilled) and at one point explicitly reported fpylll as unavailable -- no real lattice attack had ever been run against these constructions before this cert. A confounded intermediate hypothesis (short orbit period alone causes the weakness) was tested, appeared to be confirmed by one comparison, then falsified by a proper 2x2 design (period x gcd(m,3)) before arriving at the corrected mechanism above. GENERALIZATION (2026-07-04): the CRT-collapse identity (x mod m) === x (mod p) holds for any prime p dividing m, not just p=3 -- verified directly for p in {2,3,5,7,11}; it only matters here because the ternary coefficient map is fixed at mod 3, so only 3|m is relevant. Confirmed empirically against 8 moduli with long-period seed pools: m=9,24,27,81 (all 3|m) broken 5/10, 7/10, 7/10, 8/10 respectively; m=80,35,25,49 (all gcd(m,3)=1) broken 1/10, 0/10, 0/10, 0/10, matching random. CRITICAL: m=24 -- this project's own CLAUDE.md-documented 'applied' QA modulus -- is broken 7/10, more decisively than m=9 (avg ratio 0.888<1, LLL finds a vector shorter than the real key). Both of QA's two standard moduli (9, 24) are divisible by 3 and are unsafe by this construction; no existing QA convention is safe by accident. Hoffstein, Pipher, Silverman (1998) DOI:10.1007/BFb0054868. Theorem NT: QA orbit dynamics (qa_step) are integer/A1-compliant; NTRU ring and lattice arithmetic are standard integer arithmetic over the classical cryptographic construction QA orbits are fed into, not QA state itself; fpylll floating-point internals are the external attacker's tool being modeled, not a QA observer-projection concern.
